key_points: Metals are elements that are generally hard, lustrous, malleable, and ductile.
Non-metals are elements that can be solids, liquids (Bromine), or gases and are generally poor conductors of heat and electricity.
Malleability is the property by which metals can be beaten into thin sheets.
Ductility is the property by which metals can be drawn into thin wires.
Metals are sonorous, meaning they produce a ringing sound when struck by a hard object.
examples: Lustrous Metals: Gold and Silver are highly reflective and used in jewelry.
Best Conductors: Silver and Copper are excellent conductors of electricity.
Exception (Hardness): Sodium and Potassium are soft metals that can be cut with a knife.
Exception (State): Mercury is the only metal found in a liquid state at room temperature.
Non-metal conductor: Graphite (an allotrope of Carbon) is a good conductor of electricity.
formulas: Metal Oxide formation: $Metal + Oxygen ightarrow Metal\ Oxide$ (e.g., $2Cu + O_2 ightarrow 2CuO$)
Metal + Water: $Metal + Water ightarrow Metal\ Oxide + Hydrogen$ (followed by $Metal\ Hydroxide$ if soluble)
Metal + Acid: $Metal + Dilute\ Acid ightarrow Salt + Hydrogen\ gas$
exam_tips: Remember that Iodine is a non-metal but it is lustrous (shiny).
Note that Diamond is the hardest natural substance known, even though it is a non-metal (Carbon).
Identify Magnesium as the metal that burns with a dazzling white flame to form Magnesium Oxide.
Be aware that most non-metals produce acidic oxides when dissolved in water, while metals produce basic oxides.
